hypothesis: The dual momentum concept has shown genuine OOS alpha (OOS > IS Sharpe in iter 3, walk-forward consistency 1.0) but fails on trade count and per-trade edge. The root cause analysis: 8 ETFs × weekly rebalance × top-3 holding = insufficient turnover when trend filters suppress most signals. This iteration fixes this by: (1) Expanding universe from 8 to 16 ETFs (adding GLD, TLT, EEM, EFA, XLI, XLP, XLY, XLU) to double cross-sectional ranking candidates and inherently increase turnover; (2) Using bi-weekly rebalancing (every 10 days) — not daily (which failed on costs in iter 7) and not weekly (which still gave too few trades in iter 6 due to signal suppression), but with a LARGER universe making each rebalance yield more position changes; (3) Holding top 2 ETFs rather than top 3 — with 16 ETFs, top 2 represents the 87th percentile vs. 62nd percentile with 8 ETFs, making the signal more selective and higher conviction per trade; (4) Using 42-day (2-month) momentum lookback — shorter than 63 days (iter 3/6) but longer than 21 days (iter 7), balancing responsiveness vs. noise; (5) Absolute momentum filter: only hold ETF if its 42-day return > 0, no MA filter that caused signal collapse in iter 6. With 16 ETFs, bi-weekly rebalancing (26 per year × 5 IS years = 130 rebalances), and ~2 position changes per rebalance, we target 80-150 trades in IS. The larger universe also means regime diversity (bonds, gold, EM, international) reduces correlation during equity bear markets.

evaluation.post_mortem: This iteration fails on multiple critical acceptance criteria despite some positive signals. The most glaring issues are: (1) **Trade count catastrophically low**: Only 9.125 trades IS and 4.25 OOS — far below the 50-trade minimum. The expanded universe and bi-weekly rebalancing hypothesis completely failed to generate sufficient turnover; the absolute momentum filter is still suppressing signals aggressively. (2) **IS Sharpe of 0.23 is far below the 1.0 threshold** — the strategy is barely generating alpha in-sample. (3) **OOS Sharpe of `inf`** is a major red flag: with only 4.25 trades OOS, this almost certainly means zero losing trades in OOS due to statistical noise from an absurdly small sample, not genuine edge. An infinite Sharpe on 4 trades is meaningless and likely an artifact. (4) **Profit Factor (IS) of `inf`** — again, suspicious with only 9 trades; likely means zero losing trades in IS as well, which is statistically implausible over a real 5-year period and suggests data issues or the trades are so infrequent they only capture obvious trends. (5) Walk-forward only passed 4/7 windows despite a consistency score of 1.0 — the gate passed but the raw window count is mediocre. The core hypothesis of expanding the universe to generate more trades has failed: the absolute momentum filter is vetoing nearly all signals regardless of universe size. The IS/OOS comparison is essentially meaningless with sample sizes this small.
evaluation.next_direction: The absolute momentum filter must be relaxed or removed — it is the primary culprit suppressing trades to near-zero regardless of universe size. Try: (1) Remove the abs_momentum_filter entirely OR replace it with a much weaker cash filter (e.g., only go to cash if >50% of universe has negative momentum, not individual ETF level); (2) Increase top_n_held to 4-5 with the 16-ETF universe to ensure positions are always held — avoid cash; (3) Switch to daily or weekly rebalancing (rebalance_days=5) to multiply trade opportunities; (4) Consider a relative-only momentum model with no absolute filter, always holding the top-N ETFs regardless of sign — this guarantees minimum trade frequency; (5) Target 100+ trades IS before worrying about Sharpe optimization. The infinite OOS Sharpe and infinite Profit Factor are data quality/sample size artifacts that must be resolved by generating sufficient trade volume first.
